reviews.review_text:
This game does things to you man.
One day I have a blossoming Dwarven Kingdom trading in steel,
the other it gets absolutely obliterated by a singular giant spider tearing through my population through conveniently wide-built and poorly guarded and easily traversable halls.

The other day I decided to play through Adventure Mode in it's current state.
Ivor Manyannihilator. Craftsdwarf turned adventurer on a whim.
Hillocks were harassed by goblins, so I decided to take the fight to them.

I butchered 60 before I started realizing that a good 50 of them were unarmed civillians helpless against my blade.
Their corpses littered the dark fort, yet Ivor? Ivor did not care. He didn't care for anything really.
Felt nothing going into it, felt less after leaving.
He retired in the very hillocks he started in - and I spent the other day contemplating the ethics of wiping out a fortress of goblins with barely any resistance.

I've never played it as I'm too dumb to finish the tutorial.

Maybe I'll try when I have more free time

I love watching videos of people discussing it though.
This game encourages you to fantasize about drowning goblins in lava inside a rube-goldberg-esque contraption that requires at least a passing knowledge of Pascal's law.

Dwarf Fortress is the most complex game I've ever played and I find it both intimidating and endlessly fascinating. If you've ever wanted a colony builder+grand strategy+life sim+wargame, Dwarf Fortress is all of that and more, in my opinion. The endlessly entertaining emergent stories that come up in this game make it replayable in the extreme, and I'm always finding new things I'd never known about.

I wanted to give this game a try after playing Rimworld and I just can't figure out to do stuff here. The new player experience is horrible.
I guess the game has a loyal fanbase, but as a new player it is not for me.

The most disappointing experience among all games I played. It was so hyped up I really believed that this game could be good regardless all inconveniences, but, alas, it cannot.

You will have to open wiki or online disscussions to resolve every damn thing every 2 minutes because there is so little number of explanations ingame and it is extremely counterintuitive. Add a lot of bugs that still exist after 20 years of development or something (or 4 of steam I suppose). But its okay, it is developed by 2 people, if the game is good I do not care about QoL and all time I spent on trying to, for example, create ladder up from underground by trying 999 different things because it is impossible to just build ladder from underground. To be honest, it is impossible to "just do" anything in this game, but it could be managed, if you really want it.

But why to want it? Every time I tried to play DF, even 10 years ago, nothing ever happened in my fortress. Like maybe a couple of times i got "strange mood" effect on dwarfs, but that is basically it. Almost no events, nothing unique, many generic guests, but I never was even managed to find anything underground beyond ores. I suppose I can wait more than 10 hours for something to happen, mine 90 more layers, but it is the same thing every time, three times already - nothing is happening. 

Also, there is no other active maps other then your fortress. If you are sending soilders to attack anything on global map, everything happens behind curtains. I prefer rimworld approach, where you can actually see action, not just "10 people left from fortress to raid and then they just came back with message "Dwarf Stonebeard has a lot of stories to tell from this adventure"". Like ok, stories about what??? Even if there is info in the game, it is hidden under some menu in another menu in another menu that you are suppose to find somehow. 

I tried DFhack, it resolves some issues, but you still cannot check battlelog in real time and every time you open it you need to scroll it to the bottom. You still cannot know what this or that item does without constant wiki check. You still cannot know why you are sowing these plants and why exactly your dwarfs need them. All your animals still for some reason will go on the first meeting zone you will create, that is suppose to be for dwarfs, and they will be staying here forever until they starve to death unless you create separate zone for them, for every animal/type of animal individually, or for all of them together, but you will need to click on every each of it separately every time. And this zone could disappear just because you clicked somewhere in some moment and you won't even know about it until you will see all your animals together starving in the meeting hall. And so on and so on.

Maybe I will try again, but just because I am being gaslighted by thousands of people. It is really hard to believe that this kind of game have appeal of so many. Maybe because it is an original simulator of a generic fantasy world, I do not know. But I want to play the game, not simulate text in logs about towns I will never see and events I will never understand.
is a good game and is very good because so many game and game the game is game for to the game the game in the game it games very goodly it games

There's really nothing more to say that hasn't been said a thousand times about Dwarf Fortress. Buy it, you'll get your money's worth and more. This magnificient autism project deserves your patronage.

A long overdue review of what in my opinion, is one of the greatest games of all time 

I fully accept it isnt everyones cup of tea, but if you enjoy a settlement builder/story generator with a massive amount of depth and mechanics stacked upon mechanics - this may be one for you too to lose way too many hours of time playing!

I love the game has great depth of forttrss building as well as lore depth and more that the ascii art version has that i had played a bit in the past but struggled to understeand the more complex mechanics due to the ascii art style and I didnt like any of the texture packs that were out there I love this verson and the different modes.                               I would really recomend it. I have had a fort that lasted to almost 200 dwarves before accidently letting in two giant forgotten cavern beasts. Was cool exspolring the ruins with a new carivan.

I picked up Dwarf Fortress on a whim. It's not my kind of game-- I don't usually get drawn into base management/sims. But somehow, some way, Dwarf Fortress keeps me coming back. It's just plain fun. There really is no limit to what you can do, and when you think you've discovered everything, something new crawls out of the woodwork. 

My only word of warning is that this game doesn't provide much feedback on when or how things are going wrong, or how to fix them. Be prepared to have the wiki at the ready!

The classic keeps getting better!
Highly recommend to anyone who likes base management, simulation, beer, or dwarfs. 
This game has a moderately steep learning curve, so be prepared to watch a few videos or run through the in game tutorial.  
Again, this game should be in everyone's library!

This game is so awfully complex that cats get drunk because they licked their paws after stepping on spilled booze. This game has communities that share save files and collectively run a fortress into the ground in good fun. This game has forum posts theorizing how to enslave a species just to sell their expensive bones.
This game is scary. This game is fascinating.

Now it's being shipped with an adventure mode if you don't feel like wracking your brain. Just run around and stare at the stuff that the game itself is capable of doing.

I've been a fan of this game for 12 years as of now, and occasionally binge-played it several times during this time span. There's not much to say about it, because it has long ago achieved legendary status, especially among nerds like me. What I wanted to add to the discussion (praise) is that I love the direction the game is going to.

Praise to Toady One the Great who have not veered from the path! And all you guys are doing the amazing work!

Dwarf Fortress is an Absolute Masterpiece.

True appreciation for Dwarf Fortress does not emerge from a single breakthrough moment, but through the gradual mastery of its interlocking systems. It is a game that demands rigorous study and precise execution, only to reward the player with unparalleled depth. The genius of the simulation is best realized when structural progress meets unexpected catastrophe. For instance, while expanding deep underground and constructing vital infrastructure, an ancient, fire-breathing beast can decimate a heavily trained army in minutes, completely erasing hours of apparent progress.
Yet, in Dwarf Fortress, total annihilation is not a failure state; it is a narrative transition. Because the world is entirely persistent, a player can establish a new colony, train a new military, and launch a reclamation expedition back to the ruins of their former home. Returning with the tools, artifacts, and heirlooms of a past disaster transforms the gameplay into a living history, imbuing the experience with a profound sense of memory and consequence that no other game can replicate.

The game’s true power lies in its absolute dedication to systemic simulation. Mastering the mechanics of thermodynamics, geology, liquid dynamics, and raw materials unlocks a sandbox of infinite possibilities. A player can solve complex energetic and engineering problems, turning volatile environments into sustainable, impenetrable strongholds meant to stand the test of time.
The learning curve is notoriously steep, but the game remains fundamentally honest. It acts as a strict meritocracy: it deeply rewards player knowledge while ruthlessly punishing arrogance. By making the process of collapse just as engaging and intellectually stimulating as construction, Dwarf Fortress transcends the traditional boundaries of the genre. It stands as an absolute masterpiece of game design and interactive art—a monumental achievement that turns the tragedy of loss into the ultimate form of entertainment.

This game is so addictive. I really enjoyed figuring out how to play it - I did do the tutorial however, it definitely leaves a lot out. 

Play at your own peril I guess cause I've wasted a few weekends just literally being glued to my dwarves and their insanity.

This is a game meant for the inquisitive and curious, as the world you will find yourself in offers endless secrets and mysteries. For example, why would heavily armored dwarves throw themselves in a pond and drown in an attempt to kill goblins that can easily swim away? Why do dwarves traumatize themselves by not cleaning up the rotted corpse in their temple that I repeatedly tell them to take to the refuse pile? How can a child throwing a tantrum (caused by the declining mental state of seeing people die) break the legs of nearly 20 adults with no resistance? All these mysteries and more could be yours to solve through wiki deep dives after long sessions of playing in the most advanced story generator ever created.

You know this game is a masterpiece when it is displayed in The Museum Of Modern Art in New York.

See it for yourself :https://www.moma.org/collection/works/164920

Buy this game Immedeately.
This game is a known masterpiece, and words alone can't show how good it is. I'm just adding to the already 'overwhelmingly positive' pool here.
I dont play this game but still i m in love with the game, doing tutorial since infinity
Not nearly as daunting to get into as you might think. There is good reason it has been imitated by so many other games, but none have surpassed it. Play and find out why.

Love this game. The time they put in really shows. The DETAIL on everything is so enjoyable. Make sure to pause every now and then and read the datalogs on different Dwarves and ESPECIALLY the combat logs!
Great base building and resource management game. No two games are the same and there are plenty of ways to play. Faction interaction is cool and the story telling aspect is great as well!

This game is very hard to play, you have to understand the needs of the dwarves and then make sure they can get those needs from certain gameplay,,, but learning that is much of the fun.
This game really has been (for over 20 years and i used to play it back towards the start) doing the things the industry does now but years and years before. 
This game will allow you to build a fortress where you can amass hundreds (depending on your pc) of humanoids and try to keep them alive through attacks from all fantasy creatures you can imagine.
It also generates an entire fantasy world (with the history of that world) that history progresses as you play different forts so that the monsters, dragons, other fantasy tropes also all live their lives and everything is tracked and interacts down to the tiniest detail... golbins give birth, their offspring die or climb the ranks to become generals of armies that invade other places... and your fort will be the difference in these monsters being defeated or taking over the entire world.
The game really is great, hard, frustrating, and it generates stories (if you know how to track those stories).
im hoping as the game goes on it will have better interfaces for your fort and for knowing whats going on in the world around you. not that you cant do it now, but it would help to have better interfaces/experiences to really process the information. 
I cant say enough good things about this game. it has been the inspiration for so many other games and without this game the gaming space wouldnt be what it is today.
main pc - intel i9 140000k 64gig ram nvidia 4900
laptop - review was written on laptop

Picked this game up a few years ago and I return occasionally to give it another shot. 

I like learning new things and figuring things out. The dopamine hit when I finally figure out how to set up my bees for honey, or get my latest supply chain running is fun. 

That said, this game feels like an effort in futility much of the time. Losing is a big part of the experience according to the Devs, so I get what they are doing, but after 12-15 hours I need to set the game down and switch it up.

A expert focus on bug fixes and QOL changes before adding new content, NO PAID DLC with dozens of DLC level expansions to the game. Buy it, you wont regret it. 

If this was a paradox game all these new features would be 15 DLC by now. but you get it all for one price probably forever. BUY THE GAME.

Yes, there are a lot of menus and buttons, yes you're probably gonna need to visit the wiki within the first hour of play, and yeah, you may never 'win' the game. If you're looking for something to hold you hand go play something from Epic Games.

[hr][/hr]

Dwarf Fortress is a game about struggle, but in a good way! You will guide a small group of colonists to found a great dwarven empire, or die in quick and brutal ways. You will face winters, famine, spies, royalty, ghosts, goblins, and the strongest enemy -- dwarves getting depression because they had to walk into the rain for three frames.

This game builds stories that you will remember forever. You will love opening the fungal caves and pillaging its riches. You will not care about sealing it away. You will have a great big balrog come and massacre every man, woman, child, and cat in your fortress, save for one little girl who can make it out to tell the tale of your once great fortress. Losing is fun!

Allow yourself to have fun with it, remember that there's no race, and this isn't a competitive game. Its a story that you take part in.

[b] Strike the earth! [/b]

This is a legendary masterwork game encrusted with procedural history, studded with complex fluid dynamics, all craftsdwarfship is of the highest quality. It is composed of pure simulation that breathes with the lungs of a thousand civilizations, while being encircled by bands of emergent storytelling. On the item is an image of Tarn and Zach Adams the humans laboring, decorated with menacing spikes of learning curves. It features a fine engraving of a cat dying of alcohol poisoning. Beware its CPU usage.

It took me a while to leave this review I almost forgot. So for starters I am somewhat biased, So in the spirit of fighting bias I will say this game is not for everyone. To put it simply if you are a gamer that demands high octane action or superb, majestic graphic's this is not for you. If you are a gamer that does not like using your imagination or enjoy reading to understand what is going on, It may not be for you. I say reading because to see and get those cool interactions and see understand what's happening your going to have to read them on the event viewer, combat log, Legends viewer and character windows. Now to be fair it is not A LOT of reading just know actions are not played out in 3d. To keep this review short, if colony building is your thing and you do not care about 3d graphics, While enjoying cool stories and (DEEP I mean DEEP mechanics) its a good game for you. My best and last advice is to watch a in depth video of the gameplay and decide for yourself if it fits, me personally I love it and am glad Tarn Adams and his team at kitfox continue to work on this little gem. As always strike the earth urist's.

I love this game so much, I use this as the golden standard of comparison when I play other games in the genre.

There's a steep learning curve and the game constantly pushes back: settlements fail, sometimes quickly, sometimes disastrously. Definitely not a title you can pick up and master or "win" over the course of a week. But that's the beauty of it- the survival and settlement management aspects feel grounded, the story & learning feels organic, and the game is DESIGNED to be replayable and unique each playthrough.

If you picked up this game and find it frustrating, you've missed the point! Settlements fail, even when you've played the game as much as I have. Let the chaos & disasters be entertaining, brush up on a new mechanic or aspect, watch a tutorial or two, take a break to scroll through the procedurally generated history, and play again.

This game is one of if not THE MOST in depth game I have ever played. The attention to detail is crazy, there are so many aspects that impact what happens in the game that you might not ever even realise.

Never has there been, and never will there ever be a game like Dwarf Fortress. The Steam version is probably the easiest way to get into the game, as much as I have my biases and nostalgia towards the old ASCII version.

Its a bit complex, but if you ever dreamed of building moria, watching it burn to the ground, and see how it effected the history of the world, this is for you. Dwarf fortress is free, but it looks like your trying to figure out code. 
This is basically the official graphic pack, and makes the game greatly more accessible. Losing is part of the game though, so be careful if you are easily frustrated. Its very good, but extremely time consuming game.

Been playing df on and off for about 10 years now. The game is 100% in the most accessible and inviting state it's ever been in. Despite it's flaws df will always be objectively the best video game ever made. I'd recommend playing with DF hack aswell you can also download it on steam ez pz.

Bought it because i thought i could cook like in CDDA, but that is not a feature in adventure mode and modding this game is an absolute nightmare. Overall that's a pretty neat game, however if you're after survival elements then this game is not for you. 

I know this is still a work in progress, but i'm just gonna have to wait until they decide to do an overhaul in the survival aspect before i can actually play instead of testing different homebrew mods for 50 hours.

[b]Dwarf Fortress is not a game in the traditional sense, and it never communicates that clearly.[/b]

A game needs understandable rules, meaningful player agency, and systems you can learn and master. DF doesn’t offer that. Its systems are opaque, inconsistent, and often behave in ways the player cannot predict or influence. You don’t play DF. It plays you and lets you watch.

I really tried to love it, but the “work details” system broke my will to keep trying. It was added as a supposed quality‑of‑life improvement, yet instead of simplifying anything, it just layered new menus and toggles on top of the old system. You still can’t set clear rules that determine which dwarves do what, or understand what’s wrong when they don’t behave as expected. Even the most mundane tasks require a trip to the wiki or outside help.

At the core of all this is a fundamentally flawed design philosophy. Adding more layers doesn’t streamline a system; it just buries the underlying problems deeper. Real simplification would require removing what doesn’t work and clearly surfacing the remaining rules through the UI. That never happens, and I feel much of the fan base confuses opaqueness and unnecessary complexity with depth. It is not.

If you enjoy watching unpredictable stories emerge from a chaotic simulation, DF excels at that. But if you value clarity, agency, coherent rules, transparency, or the ability to set and achieve your own goals, this will feel frustrating and misleading. This is not a strategy game or a sandbox toy like most city or colony builders. It’s a simulation that hides its own rules, leaving the player guessing instead of playing.

I regret purchasing this, but I’m already 60 hours in and past the refund window.
My elderly dwarf had his arm torn off in a brutal alligator incident. He still managed to mine out half of the colony, since this was towards the start. Beautiful.

I had a surplus of stray cats that would refuse to adopt my dwarves so i built a cat cafe by overlapping my cat pasture with a tavern and now my dorfs are steadily getting cats to like them more and become pet cats 10/10
In adventure mode i cant stand if my character have status death. I think its bug. 10/10

Всегда мечтал построить собственный город или фракцию в какой-нибудь онлайн игре с реальными игроками в качестве её жителей, но каждый раз эта идея отправлялась в мусорку. 

К моему счастью, Dwarf Fortress смог частично воплотить моё желание основать поселение и делать его с каждым днём могущественней и могущественней, пусть даже управляя нпс вместо реальных игроков (потому это желание и было воплощено лишь частично, хотелось бы почувствовать в своих руках немного власти над кем-то).

Я пусть и не попробовал весь контент в этой игре, но я удовлетворён визуалом и геймплеем, а также саундтреком, музыка на фоне в этой игре прям кайфовая. А ещё я люблю дварфов, они крутые (эльфы сасать, они каннибалы и подтирают свои сраки листьями вместо того чтобы купить туалетной бумаги).


Ставлю целых 100/10. Dwarf Fortress стала моей первой игрой, которую я приобрёл в Стиме (обошлась за 700 рублей где-то, хорошая инвестиция).
I've loved this game for ages but have always been playing for free on the website and I'm happy to finally have some spare money to be able to buy a copy.

accidently dug too deep and let's just say i had to make a new fort

very fun dwarf game a must have in any dwarf chad's collection
I'm making some progress in the tutorial and it has been quite interesting so far

I genuinely feel like the only people who will truly enjoy this are people who played this years ago. It is overpriced, outdated (despite still being updates), clunky, and suffers from poor UI and QoL issues.

If you want a monumentally better game than this, I recommend Songs of Syx, with the main downside being that Dwarf Fortress lets you mine on different elevations (up/down), while Songs of Syx doesn't.
[hr][/hr]

This game has a horrendous UI, some of the worst QoL controls I've ever seen (You can place tons of structures in 1 click, but I couldn't find a way to cancel all of them so I had to manually click on each one and click the cancel button), the units move by tile hopping (no animation to go to next tile, just teleports), random sounds will play even while the game is paused like wolves howling and a sound that sounds like a cave-in, and plenty of more minor issues that just entirely put me off from even trying to build a fortress. 

I have 1800 games in my Steam library, and I am almost certain that this is the "scammiest" Colony Sim I currently own, considering its price. Its really a shame because I have literally only heard good things about this, but I'm assuming its a cult mentality or 30+ year olds who refuse to try any new games. :/
Super Replayable, with incredibly detailed simulation.

Once a guy inexplicably died in the middle of my tavern during a party and I don't know how or why. My best guess is that he slipped on vomit and bashed his skull on the floor. Also my fort is decorated with way too many statues of the dwarven god of death and decay and I once caught a child drinking vomit because we were in a water shortage. 10/10 would design a Saw Trap Pump Room that has a chance of killing everyone who uses it again

Your dwarves will fight against zombie hordes, forgotten beasts, lycanthropes, titans, and [spoilers]. They'll also fight against starvation, thirst, fatigue, poor moods, tavern brawls gone awry, dead pets, dead dwarf children, incorrect magma digs, spies, bards, aquifers, and your own human limits as you try and learn the systems to keep the fun going another season. Losing is part of the fun. An opportunity to strike the earth once more, take the lessons learned and subject a whole new population of dwarves to the vagaries of a simulation that fill the Histories of Determination and Avarice.

Dwarf Fortress is a dense and deep cavern of systems that interact in emergent ways to create an incredible experience that has defined a genre and built a following of millions of players and artists worldwide. The Steam release addresses some of the greatest learning cliffs players previously faced. The revenues brought in and expanded team at Kitfox have secured the creators' livelihood and brought consistent refinements, updates, and new features to a game that had already had a rock solid foundation.
Rock and Stone and Everything is falling apart
Quite unfortunately, Dwarf Fortress might as well be abandonware.

THE UI IS SO COMPLICATED: IF YOU WANT TO SPEND ALL YOUR TIME CONFUSED CLICKING RANDOM BUTTONS I COULDNT EVEN GET THRU THE TUTORIAL WITH MY ADHD AND I LEFT THE GAME "UP" SO I COULDN'T GET A REFUND. THIS GAME TRIGGERS MY ADHD FRUSTRATION BIG TIME AND I ALWAYS END UP RAGE QUITTING DURING THE TUTORIAL. GAME PLAY CONTROLS ARE WAY TOO COMPLICATED. I WISH ICOULD PLAY LOOKS INTERESTING.

Probably the best city builder/colony builder of all time. The amount of small details is insane, and the stories that result from them can be incredibly memorable. Honestly dont even wait for sale, these guys deserve every bit for the work they put into this

Dig Hole. Winter comes. Water freezes. Die.
Dig Hole. Dig too deep, too greedily. Die.
Dig Hole. Dig deep, carefully. Goblins raid. Die.
Nothing else like it. If you're not careful, you'll lose your life to it.
Totally worth it :) only 3 hours in and lots of fun :D
it's not for everyone but if it is for you there is a lot to like

In the first fort (that wasn't instantly killed by starvation or thirst) I played, my Military Captain was an awesome lesbian axedwarf who kicked and chopped the asses of every threat I encountered. 

Her wife was a tailor/poet and she would always perform in the tavern, which my captain adored. They even adopted some kids (who in hindsight were probably from a previous marriage) and I loved following their little family.

And then the werehog came.

Immediate chaos. It tore through a farmer who was gathering on the surface, and attacked several civilians before the army could go meet it. Despite their best efforts, one of the soldiers (not the captain, thankfully) and several civilians were bitten.

I tried everything. Walling them in, even killing the old soldier when she turned and managed to cause havoc in the sideroom of the hospital she was locked in. That, combined with the arrival of a beast/giant(I cannot for the life of me remember, all I know it killed nearly everyone) left my fortress a ruin.

The hospital was full. Even the wife of my beloved captain was in there, slowly perishing from her wounds.

My captain, oh my poor, stubborn captain, Catten was her name, was severely injured but not cursed. She could still hold an axe.

When a few more dwarves, including a child, turned into werehedgehogs, she dragged herself to the front of the hospital, and shouted "begone, fear!" before the werehogs were upon her.

Somehow, someway, maybe due to her nature or willpower or the fact her wife was behind her in the hospital, she hacked and slashed and struck the werehogs down without getting bitten.

Then, when all was quiet, she dragged herself to a hospital bed next to her wife, and collapsed.

My doctor tried their best, but neither could be saved. Catten saw the life leave her wife's eyes, and seemed to accept her end, dying mere moments after her.

I buried them together in a tomb, and made a statue in her honour.

I genuinely took a break from the game afterwards. It affected me so much that I'm sure someone will find it ridiculous, but it was one of the best tragedies I've witnessed in...anything, let alone a game.

10/10, would cry my eyes out again
